User Views Implemented by Multiple Components

A user view can be implemented by a single component or multiple components. If multiple components are used, the data can be retrieved by:

  • Components that directly access the appropriate entities of the application model
  • Invoking operations of the public component interface

Components typically represent encapsulated data. In principle, the data a component directly accesses consists of a limited number of closely related entities. If a component needs data that is 'owned' by another component, it sends the appropriate request to this component instead of directly accessing the data itself.

To understand the impact on the system load of a user view implemented by multiple components, you need to establish and combine the access profiles of the individual components. How you combine the access paths depends on the situation. The request to the other component serves as the access path that combines the access profiles of the individual components.

Consider a user view for showing orders and their prices. The user view is implemented by two components—one that retrieves orders and another that retrieves a catalog with the prices. To show the data needed, the first component retrieves the order required and its associated items directly from the database. The component then establishes the order price by invoking the second component for every order item. To do so, it repeatedly activates the operation get_price() of the second component with the appropriate signature. The signature consists of an identifier for the current material, an identifier for the vendor, a pricing date (as input parameters), and the associated price (as output parameter). The second component retrieves the relevant price information by directly accessing the database and passing the information to the first component.

The following illustration shows the access profiles for the two individual components and that the operation get_price() is activated repeatedly.

Combining the access profiles of the individual components that implement a certain user view results in a better understanding of the impact of the user view on the system load. The following illustration shows the combined access profiles of the user view for displaying orders and their prices. Note that the access path between orderline and catalog uses a dynamic link between two components instead of a relationship between two entities. The access path is depicted with a dependency symbol (a dashed arrow).

The following illustration shows the access profile of the same user view if it had been implemented by a single component.

Related Links
  • Access Profiles and Statistics

    The access profile is the sequence of all access paths needed by a user view.

  • Identify and Prioritize User Views

    A user view comprises the data needed for a business activity. The data can come from more than one modeled entity. Do not confuse the user view with the user interface of the application.
